Call 2025 Round 1 YOU
KA152-YOU - Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-1AF6C75E Deadline (Brussels Time) 12 Feb 2025 12:00:00
Project Summary
Please provide short answers to the following questions to summarise the information you have provided in the rest of the
application form.
Please use full sentences and clear language. In case your project is accepted, the summary you provided will be made
public by the European Commission and the National Agencies.
What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? What are the objectives of your project? Please specify from the
perspective of youth work practice.
The "Natural Beauties" project aims to empower young people by deepening their understanding of climate change and
equipping them with the tools and skills needed to take concrete action in their local communities. The project is designed to
bridge the gap between awareness and action by combining knowledge-sharing with hands-on environmental engagement.
Through non-formal and experiential learning, participants will not only gain theoretical insights into climate issues but also
develop practical competencies that will enable them to become active changemakers in their societies. The project is
rooted in youth work practices that emphasize peer-to-peer learning, intercultural collaboration, and real-world problem-
solving.
One of the core objectives is to help participants recognize their role in combating climate change and to inspire them to
adopt sustainable lifestyles. Many young people today are aware of environmental challenges but often lack clear guidance
on how to translate their concerns into meaningful action. This project provides a structured framework where participants
can explore sustainable practices in an interactive and supportive environment. They will engage in activities such as guided
nature explorations, community clean-up events, and sustainability workshops where they will be introduced to simple yet
impactful behavioral changes, such as reducing waste, minimizing their carbon footprint, and advocating for green policies
in their communities.
To achieve these objectives, the project integrates a variety of hands-on activities that ensure deep learning and active
participation. For example, during the "Green Exploration" activity, participants will embark on an outdoor learning
experience, where they will analyze the biodiversity of a natural area, identify environmental threats, and discuss local
conservation strategies. This experiential approach helps them understand ecological interdependencies firsthand and
supports a sense of responsibility for nature conservation. Another key activity is the "Waste Collection & Recycling
Challenge," where participants will visit a public space affected by pollution and collectively work on restoring its natural
state by cleaning up litter. They will then engage in a creative upcycling session, where they will transform discarded
materials into useful or artistic items, demonstrating the potential of recycling in an engaging and hands-on manner.
Workshops on sustainable living will also be a fundamental part of the project. Sessions will cover topics such as zero-waste
lifestyles, energy conservation, and ethical consumption. Through discussions and interactive demonstrations, participants
will learn how small changes—such as reducing single-use plastics, making informed food choices, and opting for eco-
friendly transportation—can collectively have a significant impact on the environment. They will also have opportunities to
share their own experiences and brainstorm solutions tailored to their local contexts.
Another key element of the project is nurturing youth empowerment through collaboration. Many young people feel
disconnected from environmental decision-making processes, but this project seeks to change that by providing them with
leadership opportunities. Throughout the exchange, participants will be encouraged to take ownership of specific activities,
facilitating discussions, leading group exercises, and even co-designing awareness campaigns. For instance, the "Youth for
Climate Advocacy" session will challenge participants to create short, persuasive messages that can be shared on social
media to raise awareness about climate issues. These campaigns will be published on project partners’ social media
channels, extending the project’s reach beyond the immediate participants.
Cultural exchange and teamwork are also integral to the project’s success. By bringing together young people from diverse
backgrounds, the initiative creates cross-cultural understanding and cooperation. This will be achieved through activities
like "Policy Proposal," where participants will share insights into how environmental sustainability is approached in their
home countries, exchange best practices, and explore ways to integrate global and local perspectives into their own
sustainability efforts. By doing so, they not only expand their knowledge but also build a sense of solidarity and shared
responsibility for the planet.
To ensure the long-term impact of the project, a structured follow-up strategy will be implemented. Each participant will be
encouraged to develop a personal action plan outlining concrete steps they will take upon returning home. They will also be
tasked with organizing at least one local event, such as a community clean-up, an awareness workshop, or a presentation at
a school or youth center, to share their newly acquired knowledge and experiences. This approach reinforces the principle
of knowledge multiplication.
What activities do you plan to implement? What is the number and profile of the participants involved?
The program consists of interactive, hands-on activities designed to engage participants in environmental awareness,
sustainability practices, and intercultural learning through non-formal education. It begins with preparatory activities,
including an online pre-assessment survey and a virtual introduction where participants share expectations and discuss
environmental issues in their countries. A digital platform will be created for resource sharing, and group leaders will attend
an orientation session to clarify roles and logistics.
Upon arrival, participants will take part in team-building and ice-breaking exercises to create trust and inclusion. The “Eco-

Challenge” will require them to collaborate on solving a simulated environmental crisis, encouraging problem-solving and
teamwork. This phase ensures they feel comfortable in a diverse international setting.
The core activities emphasize experiential learning. The "Green Exploration" session includes guided hikes in nature
reserves and urban green spaces, where participants assess biodiversity and environmental impact. They will learn about
conservation techniques, sustainable land management, and practical ways to protect ecosystems. This activity deepens
their connection with nature and provides real-world context for sustainability discussions.
In the “Waste Collection and Recycling” initiative, participants will collect litter from urban and natural areas, analyzing the
waste types to discuss local environmental policies. To promote circular economy principles, they will create upcycled
products from collected waste, such as reusable bags or art pieces, which will be displayed in a final exhibition. Additionally,
they will learn composting techniques and discuss waste reduction strategies.
The program includes interactive workshops on environmental advocacy, eco-entrepreneurship, and digital tools for
sustainability. Participants will explore grassroots environmental movements, analyze successful campaigns, and develop
strategies for their own activism. They will be introduced to digital mapping tools for tracking pollution and will design social
media campaigns to raise awareness about climate issues. They will have two sessions to prepare together a policy
proposal about the discussed sustainability initiatives.
A cultural exchange component will encourage knowledge-sharing about global environmental challenges. Each national
group will present an environmental issue specific to their country, followed by discussions on collaborative solutions. This
activity supports intercultural understanding and encourages participants to apply international perspectives to local
problems.
YouthPass Reflection Sessions will be held to help participants recognize their learning progress. Instead of simple
discussions, they will create digital or physical portfolios documenting their acquired skills, key insights, and post-project
action plans. This method ensures long-term impact by encouraging participants to continue applying their knowledge.
The project concludes with a community engagement event, where participants will present sustainability action plans
developed during the program. These plans will be tailored for different stakeholders (schools, businesses, local authorities)
and will be showcased at a "Sustainable Futures Fair," featuring interactive booths, demonstrations, and workshops led by
participants. This ensures that the program’s impact extends beyond its immediate participants.
The project involves 30 young people (18-30) from Hungary, Italy, Portugal, Ukraine, and Greece, with five participants and
one group leader from each country. Two experienced facilitators from Hungarian NGOs will provide mentorship and ensure
inclusive learning experiences. Special attention will be given to involving participants with fewer opportunities, ensuring
diversity and accessibility.
What results and impact do you expect your project to have?
The expected results and impact of the project are multifaceted, extending from the individual participants to local
communities, partner organizations, and even a broader European level. One of the primary outcomes is increasing young
people's awareness of environmental challenges, but beyond general knowledge, participants will engage in hands-on
activities such as waste collection, upcycling workshops, and ecological excursions. These experiences will not only provide
practical knowledge but also boost a sense of responsibility and agency in taking action for environmental protection. For
instance, by engaging in "Green Exploration" sessions, participants will have the opportunity to experience firsthand the
impact of human activity on natural ecosystems and explore real-world solutions, such as biodiversity conservation and
sustainable waste management.
At the individual level, participants will develop key competences in sustainability, leadership, teamwork, and critical
thinking. Through structured reflection sessions, such as “Self-Reflection Time” and interactive discussions like “Fears,
Expectations, Challenges,” young people will analyze their own behaviors and consider how to implement more sustainable
habits in their daily lives. These competencies will not only be recognized through the Youthpass certificate but also will
have practical applications in their personal and professional lives. A participant who becomes more conscious of waste
management, for example, may return home and start a recycling initiative in their community or influence their peers
through social media advocacy.
Beyond the direct participants, the project is designed to create a multiplier effect. Each young person will be encouraged to
share their experiences and learnings with their home communities through small-scale local initiatives. This could involve
organizing awareness campaigns, leading workshops in schools, or collaborating with local environmental organizations.
The project’s impact will be amplified through a strong dissemination plan, ensuring that best practices and lessons learned
are shared widely. In addition to posting final outcomes on social media and project websites, they will be integrated into
platforms such as SALTO, making them accessible to other NGOs, educators, and youth workers who can replicate or
adapt them in their own contexts.
At the organizational level, partner NGOs will benefit from strengthened collaboration, improved project management skills,
and new methodologies for youth engagement. The experience gained in this project will serve as a foundation for future
international collaborations, reinforcing their ability to implement high-quality youth mobility projects. The project will also
establish a network of young environmental advocates across multiple countries, facilitating continued knowledge exchange
and collaboration even after the project officially ends.
From a regional and European perspective, the project aims to contribute to a larger movement of youth-led environmental
action. By connecting participants from different cultural backgrounds, it will promote European values of cooperation,
inclusion, and active citizenship. The structured use of digital tools in dissemination, including video documentaries, blogs,
and interactive content, will ensure that the project's messages reach a broad audience. Moreover, engagement with local

municipalities, educational institutions, and environmental organizations will create lasting partnerships that can continue
addressing sustainability challenges in a more coordinated manner.
Long-term sustainability is another critical impact area. The project will establish concrete follow-up measures to ensure that
its effects do not fade after the mobility ends. Participants will be encouraged to form local eco-action groups, organize
reunions to track their progress, and contribute to an ongoing online platform where they can exchange ideas and resources.
Additionally, specific impact measurement indicators will be used to assess the behavioral changes among participants and
their communities, including tracking the implementation of sustainable habits and the creation of new environmental
initiatives.
In essence, the project is designed not only to educate but also to inspire action, equipping young people with the skills,
motivation, and network to drive real environmental change. Through a combination of practical engagement, reflective
learning, and strategic dissemination, it will support a new generation of young leaders dedicated to sustainability, ensuring
that the impact extends far beyond the duration of the project itself.

Background and experience
Please briefly present your organisation/the group.
The Street of Youth Association is a youth organization based in Budapest, Hungary, and composed of Generation Z
members, specifically focusing on the challenges, problems, and issues faced by Generation Z youth (18-25 years old
youngsters). Our specialty is that we achieve this primarily by focusing on international projects and non-formal education.
We believe in the power of non-formal education and in the power of Erasmus+. We understand that Gen Z young people
are greatly helped through these project, especially with starting their careers, finding their path, and developing their soft
skills. Our members are exclusively Generation Z youth who want to contribute to their own community and support other
young people. In the association, both the management and the local programs, as well as the international projects, are
carried out by young people, for young people. We believe this is the most effective way to truly make an impact on the lives
of other young people, engage with them and have a positive influence on our environment. We can identify with the fact that
this generation has completely different needs and is driven by different topics compared to previous generations.
Accordingly, there are three topics that we prioritize and consider the most important in our organization: the mental health
of Gen Z young people; environment protection and sustainability; and the challenges related to the digital world, particularly
social media. We also believe in youth empowerment. It is important to mention that our association operates on a similar
principle to a startup: if any of our members have a good idea or a passion project, the Street of Youth Association provides
the opportunity and a supportive environment for them to realize it.
What are the activities and experience of the organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your organisation’s
/ group’s regular youth work activities.
Although we are a young association, established only in February 2023, our members, who are mostly university students
and young professionals, have significant experience as participants in Erasmus+ youth exchanges, training courses, and
youth participation projects. We are working to utilize our experience and knowledge to create high-quality non-formal
learning mobility opportunities. Street of Youth Association is a newcomer organization in Erasmus+. However, we are
currently working hard on projects in the previously mentioned topics, and we want to tackle the challenges we have
experienced with other young people and make an impact on our environment and young peers through non-formal learning.
Regarding the mental health of Gen Z young people, we particularly focus on work-life balance, the challenges around
starting careers, and excessive online presence. For the latter, a podcast series is in the pipeline, led by young people and
featuring experts, which is currently in the planning phase. Regarding environmental protection and sustainability, the topics
of climate anxiety, sustainable habits, and climate activism are of particular concern to our organization and members. We
are currently preparing an Erasmus+ youth exchange aimed at developing sustainable habits, which may take place in
2025. Regarding the challenges arising from digitalization among young people, our focus is on the conscious use of social
media, mindful media consumption and critical thinking, as well as screen time management. We place special emphasis on
organizing as many local programs as possible, or our members and other interested young people so that members can
connect offline, such as hiking together, playing soccer, and board games. As well as promoting Erasmus+, Visegrad, and
other international short-term mobility programs, to provide young people with broad opportunities to participate in these
initiatives. We have noticed that young people have limited access to these opportunities, and many are not even aware of
them. We want to democratize this as much as possible and enable more young people to explore Europe, connect with
their European peers, and learn about topics that concern them through non- formal opportunities. We pay special attention
to social media in this regard.
Please give information on the key staff/persons involved in this application and on the competences and previous
experience that they will bring to the project.
President – Levente Fórián - BSc in International Business at BME, Masters in Finance at ELTE - Ran a startup for over a
year after winning a competition and got a fund for starting - Active in nature with outdoor activities as well as hikes and trips
- Managing partnerships and ongoing projects at local and international level as well - Organizing and participating in local
activities of the NGO - Participated in many Erasmus+ project experiences Vice-President – Gergő Fazekas - BSc in
International Business at BME and in Programming at ELTE, Masters in Finance at ELTE - Continuously helps the NGO
with IT related tasks and issues - Responsible apart from IT, for future projects capacity planning, organizing and
participating in local activities and events - Active in fields of cultural and political dialogues as well as entrepreneurship -
Active organiser and member of sport events in local level Project manager - Bence Bíró - BSC in Business and
Management at BME and International Economics MSC at Corvinus University of Budapest - Former startupper, Hungarian
Startup University Program finalist - Experience in various youth NGOs as a volunteer - Experience with over 20+ Erasmus+
projects as participant - Facilitated 2 Erasmus+ youth exchange projects - Non-formal learning enthusiast

Background and experience
Please briefly present the organisation / the group
Romagna+ is an Italian Youth organization with a legal form of association, that operates in the field of Erasmus+ projects to
reach important objective We always look forward to reaching our goals, which are: (cid:0) Creating conditions for the active
involvement of young people in the social, economic, and cultural life of society (cid:0) Encourage young people to take part in
Erasmus+ projects (cid:0) Develop awareness of the need for change (cid:0) Empower sustainability (cid:0) Giving the opportunities to
connect with others from different countries (cid:0) Creation of an inclusive and tolerant environment (cid:0) Developing international
youth cooperation, to cooperate with foreign youth organizations The organization always tries to be coherent with its
cornerstones, which are: (cid:0) Active participation in social and political life, especially at the European level (cid:0) Setting an
international and modern mindset to be active citizens in the world (cid:0) Doing our best to make our planet a better place,
through sustainability, tolerance, and inclusion. We strive to make people feel part of the Union, to sensibilize the importance
each one of us has as a citizen. A small change by an individual can have a strong impact on the society. We aim to make
everyone aware of this, to contribute to the surrounding environment and society. Romagna+ always looks to the future,
strongly believing in the importance of young people, precisely because they will shape the forthcoming planet. We do our
best to sensibilize these generations on themes like sustainability, inclusion, tolerance, equality, innovation, and respect,
with many instruments: among those the most important and with the most impact are Youth Exchanges, Training Courses,
DiscoverEU for Inclusion, Youth Participation Activities, Local Events and Cooperation Partnerships. The idea of creating
this organization evolved during a Training Course in Denmark, where three members out of four took part. The desire to
create our organization has been present for a long time for each of us since we are all active participants in the Erasmus+
program. For this reason, when we talked about this, we all agreed to get involved in this challenge. First, there was the
common perception that an Erasmus+ organization was needed in our area, but mostly, we felt the need to give us
contribute, involving more youngsters, and making them aware of what the EU offers them, which is still not well-known in
Italy. The group was originally composed of four students at Bologna University. Now the team grew and Romagna+ has 41
associates, 24 actively working on projects, administration and team development. Our organization always looks forward to
getting better and catching every growth opportunity, both at an international and local level, enhancing the talent of people
inside and outside of the group. We founded our company with a common European mindset, and we strive to keep this
attitude, passing these values to all those people we might get in contact with during our journey.

organisation’s / group’s regular youth work activities at local level.
Romagna+ was founded in October 2020, by four young European citizens, ready and willing to make a difference in a
European context, exploiting their individual previous experiences. All the members of the team were involved in the field of
youth both und the Erasmus+ program and outside of it. We have taken part to several projects: Youth Exchanges, Training
Courses, European Solidarity Corps, and the Erasmus Mundus program, trying to differentiate the topics we were
deepening, but always in line with our interests, which we usually share. Working with youngsters of different ages we had
the chance to develop many approaches, which we could apply to this program, giving added value and various points of
view on how to face a topic, all in the non-formal education context, as the skills we developed are from. We focus on
developing a national and international network, taking part in associations at local and European levels. All the members
are involved at least in a local association, showing high engagement in society, especially for a young age. Each of us had
also the opportunity to enhance our own international experience, as a matter of fact, other than YE and TC, all the active
members spent a semester or more abroad, for studies, internships, and work experiences. Living and visiting so many
countries helped in enlarge their mind boundaries, being ready and open to accept and learn from different cultures. As an
organization, we held our first Youth Exchange in Italy, called “Make Sustainable Change” in April 2022, and another one in
March 2024 called “Everyone. Every day. Equal.”, and in August 2024 we hosted our fir Training Course “Spot The Hoax”,
in June 2025 we will hold another one called “Beat the Neets”, and in September 2025 we will hold a Youth Exchange called
“Reconnecting”. Our main focus topics are: sustainability, gender equality, youth employability, misinformation, mental
health and inclusion. In 2022, 2023 and 2024 we were partners in several projects and training courses, with an increasing
number of collaborations year over year. Romagna+ merges open-minded and internationally oriented people, with expertise
that spaces in different areas, deriving from studies and practical activities. The members have developed knowledge
through experiences in various areas, such as sports, volunteering, young and adult education and entertainment, support
for homeless people, sustainable practices, and inclusion of people with fewer opportunities. The participants study, either
academically or personally, different subjects, which space from international relations, marketing, management, business,
mathematics, law, green sciences, sport and physical education, and so forth. All these competencies will be put into
practice, to reach the best output from every activity the Romagna+ will face.
Please give information on the key staff/persons involved in this application and on the competences and previous

Project Rationale
In this section, you are asked to explain the aims of your project, what you want to achieve and what are its expected
benefits on participants and participating organisations. Make sure to read the questions carefully and address all sub-
questions.
Needs and Objectives
Why do you want to carry out this project? Please describe the issues and needs you want to address and your project’s
objectives.
The motivation to carry out this project stems from the growing urgency of addressing climate change and environmental
degradation, issues that have become increasingly evident in recent years. The idea for this initiative emerged from
discussions within our NGO, where members expressed concerns about rising temperatures not only during summer but
throughout the entire year. This naturally led to conversations about air pollution, one of the primary contributors to climate
change, and the broader impact of human activities such as deforestation, urbanisation, and industrialisation. These
discussions highlighted a crucial gap in awareness—many individuals, especially young people, are not fully aware of the
extent to which their daily actions contribute to environmental degradation or what they can do to mitigate these effects.
Recognising this, we looked into existing data to better understand public perceptions of environmental challenges.
According to the Multisurvey 'Aspects of Daily Life' conducted by ISTAT between 2012 and 2021, climate change was
identified as the most pressing environmental concern by more than half (51.5%) of individuals aged 14 and over in 2021.
Air pollution followed closely behind, yet concern over these issues has slightly declined in larger municipalities in the past
three years. Waste disposal and production were also noted as significant concerns, with 44.1% of respondents expressing
worry, followed by water pollution at 40.1%, and the greenhouse effect and ozone depletion at 34.9%. Meanwhile, issues
such as noise pollution and landscape degradation ranked lower, affecting fewer than 30% of respondents. These statistics
underscore the fact that while environmental concerns are widely acknowledged, there remains a disparity between
awareness and proactive engagement in addressing these challenges.
Despite the widespread recognition of climate-related issues, our assessment revealed a lack of structured needs analysis
among our target group. While members of our organisation expressed interest in tackling environmental challenges, a
formal needs assessment involving a broader audience was not conducted prior to the development of this project. This
highlights a critical shortfall—without directly engaging young people in identifying their specific concerns, knowledge gaps,
and motivations, interventions may not fully address their needs or encourage lasting behavioral change. A structured needs
assessment would provide valuable insights into the level of environmental awareness among young people, the barriers
they face in adopting sustainable practices, and the types of activities that would most effectively engage them in climate
action.
To bridge this gap, the 'Natural Beauties' project aims to boost environmental awareness and action through non-formal and
informal education. By engaging young people in discussions about the impact of human activities on nature, we hope to
cultivate a deeper understanding of environmental issues and encourage proactive behaviors. The project will facilitate
hands-on activities, including practical workshops, discussions, and collaborative initiatives, enabling participants to
translate their awareness into tangible actions. Sessions such as 'Taking Care of the Environment' will focus on pollution
and solutions to mitigate its effects, while reflection activities will provide participants with an opportunity to consider their
personal contributions to environmental sustainability.
One of the fundamental objectives of the project is to instill a sense of responsibility and agency in young people. While
climate change is a global issue, local actions play a crucial role in mitigating its effects. Through participation in this project,
young people will not only gain knowledge about environmental challenges but also develop skills and strategies for
integrating sustainable practices into their daily lives. Additionally, by bringing together participants from different countries,
the project creates cross-cultural exchange and cooperation, reinforcing the idea that environmental protection is a
collective responsibility that transcends national boundaries.
Ultimately, this project seeks to empower young people to become active participants in climate action. By equipping them
with knowledge, tools, and a supportive community, we aim to enable them to make informed choices and inspire change
within their local contexts. Addressing the identified gap in needs assessment, we will also incorporate participant feedback
mechanisms throughout the project lifecycle to ensure that activities remain relevant and impactful. This approach aligns
with the broader goals of the Erasmus+ programme, which emphasizes lifelong learning and youth empowerment as key
drivers of social and environmental change.
How does your project link to the objectives of the Erasmus programme and those of Youth Exchanges?
The project aligns closely with the objectives of the Erasmus+ programme and the Youth Exchange framework by nurturing
intercultural dialogue, promoting active citizenship, and empowering young people through non-formal education. Youth
exchanges provide an invaluable opportunity for young people to step beyond their local environments and engage with
peers from diverse cultural and socio-economic backgrounds, building mutual understanding and creating a sense of
European identity. Our project, Natural Beauties, leverages these opportunities by bringing together participants from
Europe, enabling them to connect, collaborate, and develop both personal and professional skills in an international setting.
One of the core goals of Erasmus+ is to enhance young people's awareness of socially and environmentally relevant issues
and inspire them to take action. Climate change is a challenge that transcends borders and requires a collective response,

Project Design
Preparation, support and follow-up
How will you prepare the participants before the start of the activity (e.g. intercultural, linguistic, risk-prevention etc.) and
how will you support them during and after the activity?
Before the activity, we will ensure that participants are fully prepared through structured pre-departure arrangements, a
comprehensive Online Advanced Planning Visit (APV), and continuous support throughout and after the exchange. The
APV, held one month before the exchange, will involve team leaders, facilitators, and at least one participant per country.
This session will cover all logistical, cultural, and safety aspects, ensuring that both practical and learning objectives are
clear. Participants will receive guidance on travel, accommodation, insurance, risk management, and emergency
procedures. A detailed risk-prevention plan will be shared, including conflict resolution, cultural sensitivity, ethical behavior,
and personal safety tips. The APV will also finalize the Project Info Pack, which will include the activity schedule, travel
guidance, Youthpass information, emergency contacts, and project expectations.
To promote early engagement, we will create WhatsApp and Facebook groups where participants introduce themselves,
share their motivations, and get familiar with the project. A language support plan will be implemented to help participants
with lower English proficiency. We will provide an essential language toolkit and set up a buddy system, pairing weaker
English speakers with more experienced peers for support. Additionally, an intercultural awareness program will be
conducted before departure, helping participants understand cultural norms, differences, and potential challenges in an
international group.
Each participant will complete a personal needs form, ensuring dietary preferences, medical conditions, and special learning
needs are addressed. We will also prepare a cultural adaptation guide, covering local customs, do’s and don’ts, and
expected behavior. To create an inclusive and diverse atmosphere, room allocations will ensure participants from different
countries share accommodations, nurturing deeper intercultural bonds.
Support During the Activity
Upon arrival, a Welcome and Orientation Session will take place, introducing participants to the venue, project goals, house
rules, and key safety contacts. To create a positive and cooperative environment, team-building activities and ice-breaking
exercises will take place immediately, allowing participants to form connections and feel at ease. Every participant will be
assigned a support mentor, either a facilitator or team leader, to provide guidance if challenges arise. A daily well-being
check-in system will be in place, where team leaders and facilitators assess participants' emotional, social, and learning
experiences, ensuring they feel safe, included, and engaged.
Non-formal learning methods such as role-playing, interactive discussions, and outdoor activities will encourage deeper
engagement. A crucial element will be Reflection Time, which will be conducted daily to allow participants to process their
experiences, set learning goals, and track personal development. These reflections will contribute to their Youthpass
certification, documenting their acquired skills.
Post-Project Follow-Up and Long-Term Engagement
After the activity, participants will continue to receive structured support. A post-exchange online debriefing session will be
held two weeks later to help participants reflect on their experiences, discuss key takeaways, and explore ways to integrate
their learning into daily life. National groups will be encouraged to conduct local dissemination activities, such as
presentations, community discussions, or sustainability initiatives, to spread their newly gained knowledge. The host
organization will provide materials and support for these activities.
To maintain engagement, participants will contribute to a joint digital storytelling project, where they document their learning
journey through blog posts, social media content, and video reflections, which will be shared across Erasmus+ platforms.
This will ensure broader outreach and allow future participants to learn from their experiences. We will also establish an
alumni network, inviting past participants to stay involved in Erasmus+ initiatives and mentor new participants.
Through these structured preparation, support, and follow-up strategies, participants will not only maximize their learning
and intercultural experience but also develop long-term engagement with sustainability and youth initiatives beyond the
project’s formal timeline.
What measures will you put in place to ensure the safety and protection of participants?
Ensuring the safety and protection of participants is a top priority for our project. To achieve this, we have developed a
comprehensive safety plan covering all aspects of the participants’ well-being, including physical safety, health measures,
psychological support, emergency preparedness, and safeguarding against potential risks.
From the moment participants arrive, they will be provided with a detailed safety briefing that outlines key rules and
procedures. This will include emergency protocols, contact details for organizers and local authorities, and a clear
explanation of behavioral expectations to create a safe and inclusive environment. Each participant will receive an
information pack with all safety-related details, including maps, emergency contact numbers, addresses of nearby medical
facilities, and guidelines on local customs and laws.
Accommodation has been carefully selected based on stringent safety requirements. The facility meets fire and health
safety standards, is equipped with emergency exits, fire extinguishers, and a first aid station, and has 24/7 security
measures in place. Additionally, a member of the organizing team will be available at all times to provide assistance and
oversee the well-being of participants. The accommodations are located within reasonable proximity to hospitals, clinics,
and pharmacies to ensure that medical assistance is readily available if needed.
During travel and transportation, safety is ensured by organizing group journeys rather than allowing individuals to travel

routes, schedules, and safe traveling practices. A designated responsible person from the team will accompany participants
on all organized trips to ensure no one gets lost and to provide immediate support if needed. For additional security, a local
native speaker will be present to help with communication barriers and provide assistance in case of misunderstandings or
emergencies.
Before arrival, participants will be required to complete a health declaration form, disclosing any pre-existing conditions,
allergies, or required medications. Organizers will then ensure that necessary medical facilities and pharmacies are
accessible. First aid kits will be available at all activity locations, and at least one trained first aider will be present at all
times. Moreover, participants will be advised to obtain adequate health insurance coverage, including travel and accident
insurance. For EU citizens, having a European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) is strongly encouraged to facilitate access to
medical services.
Emergency preparedness is a key component of our safety strategy. Each participant will be briefed on emergency
response procedures, including evacuation plans, fire safety measures, and what to do in case of an accident. Emergency
contact numbers for medical services, police, and fire departments will be clearly displayed in common areas and activity
spaces. A dedicated vehicle will be on standby for urgent medical transport if required.
For the duration of the project, all activities have been carefully designed to minimize risks. Outdoor and physical activities,
such as gardening, hiking, or waste collection, will be supervised by trained professionals. Participants will be provided with
necessary protective equipment, including gloves, appropriate footwear, and sun protection. In cases of extreme weather
conditions, alternative indoor activities will be arranged to avoid any risk of exposure to harsh elements.
Mental well-being is just as important as physical safety. To support participants' psychological health, the program includes
mindfulness and relaxation sessions, opportunities for self-reflection, and designated quiet spaces where individuals can
unwind. If a participant experiences emotional distress, our team members, trained in basic mental health first aid, will be
available for support and guidance. Additionally, participants will have the opportunity to provide anonymous feedback
regarding their well-being throughout the program, allowing organizers to address any concerns promptly.
A strict safeguarding policy will be enforced to ensure that all interactions are respectful and that every participant feels
secure. Clear guidelines will be in place to prevent harassment, bullying, or any form of discrimination. A confidential
reporting mechanism will be established so that participants can raise concerns without fear of retaliation. The organizing
team will also conduct regular check-ins with participants to ensure their comfort and safety.
Finally, communication will be maintained at all times through a group messaging system where updates, reminders, and
emergency alerts can be shared instantly. This will allow organizers to quickly address any arising issues and keep
participants informed about any necessary safety measures.
What activities are foreseen after the end of the Youth Exchange? How will the participants follow-up on the activity?
After the end of the Youth Exchange, several structured follow-up activities will ensure that the impact of the project extends
beyond the participants and the immediate project duration. Each participating organisation will be responsible for guiding
their respective groups through a well-defined dissemination and engagement plan that reinforces the lessons learned and
maintains the momentum of the exchange.
Participants will take an active role in sharing their experiences within their local communities, educational institutions, and
youth organisations. To achieve this, they will organise thematic workshops, storytelling sessions, and panel discussions at
schools, universities, and community centres. These events will not only highlight their newly acquired knowledge on
sustainability and Erasmus+ opportunities but also encourage other young people to engage in similar initiatives. The
participating organisations will provide logistical support, materials, and guidance to ensure the effectiveness of these
activities.
One of the key follow-up activities will be a peer-to-peer mentoring initiative, where participants will mentor younger peers or
new volunteers in their organisations. This will help them apply their knowledge in a real-world setting and enhance their
leadership, communication, and organisational skills. Additionally, they will be encouraged to use creative tools such as
podcasts, blogs, or short documentary-style videos to share their insights in an engaging and accessible format.
Each participant group will be tasked with designing a small-scale environmental initiative in their home community. This
could involve activities like local clean-up campaigns, sustainability awareness sessions, or the creation of urban green
spaces. These initiatives will serve as a practical way to reinforce their commitment to sustainability while creating
community engagement. The partner organisations will provide support by helping participants connect with local
stakeholders, securing necessary permissions, and sourcing materials or funding where needed.
To ensure that the impact of the project is measurable, participants will complete individual reflection reports and feedback
surveys detailing how they have applied their learning since the exchange. They will also be invited to contribute to a
collective impact report, which will be compiled and published online. This report will highlight key learnings, best practices,
and recommendations for future youth projects.
A follow-up online networking meeting will be held three months after the project ends, bringing together all participants and
partner organisations. This will serve as a platform for sharing progress updates, discussing challenges faced during local
activities, and identifying opportunities for future collaboration. This meeting will also be used to plan the submission of
future Erasmus+ project proposals based on the participants’ ideas and interests developed during the exchange.
Social media engagement will remain a crucial part of the follow-up strategy. Participants will be encouraged to regularly
post about their follow-up activities using a common hashtag, allowing for real-time tracking of their impact. Dedicated
content such as vlogs, interviews, and “day-in-the-life” features of participants implementing sustainability in their daily lives
will be shared across platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and Facebook to maintain visibility. Partner organisations will also

Recognition of learning outcomes
How will you support participants to be aware of what they have learned and which competences they have developed or
improved? Please remember to include the methods that support reflection and documentation of the learning outcomes in
the daily timetable of each activity.
Throughout the project, we will ensure that participants are fully aware of their learning progress, skill development, and
competence improvement by integrating structured reflection, interactive learning documentation, and YouthPass validation
into the daily schedule. The recognition of learning outcomes will not only be a closing step at the end of the exchange but
will be a continuous, dynamic process that supports self-awareness, personal growth, and peer support.
To begin with, participants will be introduced to the concept of YouthPass on the very first day. A dedicated session will
clarify its purpose as a tool for recognizing and validating non-formal and informal learning within Erasmus+ projects.
Instead of passively receiving information, participants will actively engage in identifying the eight key competences that
YouthPass focuses on: Multilingual competence, Personal, social, and learning-to-learn competence, Citizenship
competence, Entrepreneurship competence, Cultural awareness and expression competence, Mathematical competence
and competence in science, technology, and engineering (STEM), Literacy competence, and Digital competence. They will
work in small groups using interactive methods such as role-playing, debates, and brainstorming sessions to connect these
competences to their personal goals and project activities.
From day one, participants will be encouraged to set personal learning goals related to these competences. To make this
process more engaging and accessible, we will introduce individual learning diaries or reflection journals where participants
will write their expectations, track their progress, and note key insights. These journals will serve as a private space for self-
reflection, helping them structure their thoughts and recognize their improvements.
Every evening, we will hold a structured "Reflection Circle," which will be an integral part of the daily routine. This session
will not only allow participants to summarize what they have learned during the day but will also encourage them to link their
learning outcomes to the key competences outlined in YouthPass. The Reflection Circle will be guided by facilitators using
targeted questions that challenge participants to think critically about their experiences. Some examples of these reflective
prompts include:
"What was the most surprising thing you learned today?"

Environmental sustainability will be at the core of this project, ensuring participants gain both theoretical knowledge and

approach to green living.
To reduce the carbon footprint of travel, participants will be encouraged to use trains and buses where feasible, while those
who must fly will be informed about carbon offsetting programs. Upon arrival, all in-country travel will prioritize walking,
biking, or public transportation. Short-distance travel between venues will be incorporated into the program through guided
"Walk & Talk" discussions on sustainability. Carpooling will be arranged when necessary, ensuring minimal vehicle use.

controlling portion sizes, we will implement a "Zero-Waste Kitchen Challenge," requiring participants to plan and cook meals
using locally sourced, seasonal ingredients while minimizing waste. Meals will emphasize plant-based foods to reduce the
environmental impact of meat consumption. Any leftover food will be shared or repurposed into future meals, and a food-
sharing station will allow participants to redistribute any surplus. The "Local Dinners" activity will highlight farm-to-table
principles by using homegrown produce from the gardening workshops, where participants will plant and nurture herbs and
vegetables for consumption.
Waste management will follow a strict "Zero Single-Use Plastic" policy. No disposable bottles, cutlery, or cups will be
provided. Instead, each participant will receive a reusable water bottle, cloth napkin, and tote bag. Waste segregation will be
enforced, with color-coded bins available at all venues. A friendly "Eco Challenge" will reward participants who reduce
waste, compost food scraps, and upcycle materials. Creative workshops will repurpose discarded items into useful
products, reinforcing sustainability through hands-on engagement.
Resource efficiency will also be prioritized. A paperless policy will be implemented using Google Drive for tickets, schedules,
and learning materials, eliminating unnecessary printing. Energy and water conservation practices will be promoted, with
reminders to turn off lights, take shorter showers, and reuse towels and linens. During accommodation, participants will be
encouraged to track their daily energy and water use, nurturing long-term awareness.

Daily reflection sessions will include sustainability check-ins, encouraging participants to evaluate their eco-friendly choices.
Engaging debates and role-playing activities will challenge them to think critically about global environmental issues and
develop practical solutions. At the end of the program, participants will create a "Sustainability Commitment Plan," outlining
eco-friendly habits they pledge to continue post-exchange. These commitments will be compiled into a digital resource,
shared through partner organizations and social media to inspire broader change.
By integrating sustainability into every facet of the program, participants will leave with the skills and motivation to adopt
greener lifestyles. The project’s impact will extend beyond its duration, as participants bring sustainable practices to their
communities, nurturing long-term environmental consciousness.

Evaluation
How will you evaluate your project’s success? Which activities will you carry out in order to assess whether, and to what
extent, your project has reached its objectives and results?
We will evaluate our project’s success through a structured, multi-layered approach that integrates qualitative and
quantitative methods, ensuring a thorough assessment at the participant, partner organization, and community levels. The
evaluation will measure learning outcomes, organizational development, and long-term impact, while also ensuring
continuous feedback mechanisms.
At the participant level, we will focus on knowledge acquisition, skill development, and behavioral changes. Before the
project, participants will complete baseline self-assessment surveys, evaluating their initial understanding of environmental
issues, sustainability, and teamwork. These will include both Likert-scale and open-ended questions to capture qualitative
insights. During the project, we will conduct daily reflection sessions, where participants discuss their learning progress,
challenges, and key takeaways. These discussions will be structured around guided questions to ensure focused
reflections. In addition, peer evaluations will be introduced, where participants provide structured feedback to one another
on competencies such as leadership, collaboration, and problem-solving. This will be facilitated through activities like
“strengths and improvements” feedback circles, where participants highlight one strength and one area for development for
each peer. To ensure honest feedback, we will also conduct anonymous digital surveys at key project milestones, allowing
participants to express their opinions freely about the effectiveness of activities, facilitators, and overall learning
environment.
To measure the practical application of learning outcomes, participants will engage in group projects that involve designing
sustainability campaigns or local action plans. These will serve as a concrete demonstration of their acquired knowledge
and will be assessed based on creativity, feasibility, and potential impact. The Youthpass certification process will be
another key tool for evaluating participants' learning progress. Participants will document their key learnings and reflect on
how their competencies have improved. The facilitators will guide them in linking these newly developed skills to future
opportunities, such as advocacy work, academic pursuits, or professional development.
At the partner organization level, we will evaluate the effectiveness of collaboration, communication, and organizational
learning. Before the project, each partner will define their expectations and key objectives, which will be revisited during the
evaluation phase. Throughout the project, we will hold weekly coordination meetings, where partners assess logistical
effectiveness, participant engagement, and emerging challenges. These discussions will be documented in a shared digital
workspace, ensuring that feedback is continuously incorporated.
After the project, each partner organization will submit a structured impact report, evaluating how their organization has
benefited from the project, what new capacities have been developed, and any challenges faced. The final partner
evaluation meeting will allow organizations to discuss their findings collectively, highlighting best practices and areas for
future improvement. We will also conduct a six-month follow-up evaluation, where partners report on any long-term effects,
such as new project ideas, continued partnerships, or improvements in their internal processes.
For broader community impact, we will evaluate dissemination effectiveness and real-world applications of project
outcomes. Each participant will be encouraged to lead a local sustainability initiative in their community, such as awareness
campaigns, social media advocacy, or school presentations. These activities will be tracked through follow-up surveys and
testimonials, assessing whether participants continue applying their skills post-project. We will also monitor engagement
metrics on social media, analyzing the reach, interactions, and discussions generated by project-related content. This will
provide a measurable indicator of how widely the project's message has spread beyond direct participants.
To ensure long-term impact measurement, we will conduct three-month and six-month follow-ups with participants to track
whether they have remained active in sustainability efforts, started new initiatives, or applied their skills in personal or
professional contexts. These follow-ups will include both surveys and case study interviews, capturing success stories and
challenges in maintaining project impact. The results of these evaluations will be compiled into a final impact report,
summarizing key findings, best practices, and recommendations for future projects.
